objections.description: The artifact is truncated mid-sentence in Section 10.3 (Document Detail / Timeline Screen). The TimelineItem specification, the entire ActionSheet/Form screen (10.4), and presumably remaining sections (accessibility compliance details, performance budgets, testing strategy, migration rollback plan) are missing. This is a convergence deliverable — shipping an incomplete spec means downstream agents (ZAI, Kimi, Qwen) cannot approve component structure, accessibility compliance, or performance characteristics as required by convergence criteria.
objections.suggested_fix: Complete Section 10.3 (TimelineItem populated/empty/error states, interaction details), add full Section 10.4 for ActionSheet/ActionForm screen (all four states, form validation, submission flow, error handling, keyboard/screen-reader behavior), and add remaining sections: Section 11 (Accessibility — focus management, ARIA patterns per component, keyboard navigation matrix, screen reader announcements), Section 12 (Performance budget — bundle size targets, LCP/FID/CLS goals, lazy loading thresholds), Section 13 (Testing strategy — what gets unit tested, integration tested, and how), Section 14 (Migration rollback plan — how to revert to Alpine if React deploy fails).

objections.description: The Suspense boundary for lazy-loaded screens is never specified. React.lazy is declared in 5.3 for SearchScreen, DocumentScreen, and ActionSheet, but the composition hierarchy in 5.2 shows no <Suspense> wrapper with a fallback. Without it, the lazy imports will throw when the chunk hasn't loaded yet. The fallback cannot be a spinner (per constraints), so the skeleton state needs to be explicitly wired as the Suspense fallback per screen.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a <Suspense fallback={<ScreenSkeleton />}> wrapper around <Router> in the composition hierarchy. Define ScreenSkeleton as a generic full-screen skeleton using SkeletonBlock that matches the target screen's rough layout. Alternatively, use per-route Suspense boundaries so each screen gets its own skeleton shape as fallback.

objections.description: Building a custom hash router from scratch (~60 lines claim) that handles path params, query params, history stack, scroll restoration, aria-live announcements, and focus management is a significant liability. The claim of ~60 lines is unrealistic for production-grade behavior including edge cases (double-hash in URLs, encoded params, concurrent navigation, popstate vs hashchange). This will be the #1 source of bugs. If the constraint is 'no external routing library', the spec needs to acknowledge the complexity and specify exact edge case handling. If there's no such constraint, use a battle-tested minimal router.
objections.suggested_fix: Either (a) specify that a lightweight router like wouter (3.5KB) is acceptable and use its hash-based mode, or (b) expand the Router spec to include: URL encoding/decoding strategy, handling of malformed hashes, behavior when hash contains query params with special characters, guard against navigation during pending async operations, and exact unit test cases for the router. The ~60 lines estimate needs to be either proven with pseudocode or corrected.

objections.description: The catch-all route `/{path:path}` will conflict with existing API routes depending on FastAPI route registration order. If the catch-all is registered after API routes, it works. If registered before (or if FastAPI's path matching is ambiguous for paths like `api/search` vs `{path:path}`), API calls will get index.html instead of JSON. The spec doesn't define route registration order or verify that existing web_server.py mounts API routes first.
objections.suggested_fix: Explicitly state that the catch-all MUST be the last route registered. Better: add an explicit `path.startswith('api/')` check that returns 404 (already shown in code), AND add a `path.startswith('assets/')` passthrough. Also specify that the static mount for `/assets` must be registered before the catch-all. Include a smoke test: `curl /api/search?q=test` must return JSON, not HTML.

objections.description: Light theme `--color-text-secondary: #6E6E73` on `--color-bg-primary: #FFFFFF` has a contrast ratio of approximately 4.6:1. The spec claims this 'meets AA for 16px+' but WCAG AA for normal text (<18.66px bold / <24px regular) requires 4.5:1. At 16px regular weight, this is normal text, so 4.6:1 barely passes. However, at 14px (`--text-sm`, used extensively for snippets and metadata), this is a WCAG AA failure. The secondary text color will be used on sm text throughout search results and document meta.
objections.suggested_fix: Darken `--color-text-secondary` in light theme to at least `#636366` (contrast ~5.5:1 on white) to safely pass AA at all body text sizes. Alternatively, enforce in the spec that `--color-text-secondary` is never used below `--text-base` (16px) and introduce a `--color-text-secondary-strong` for smaller text sizes.

objections.location: artifact:section-7 (State Management - no loading cancellation on unmount)
objections.description: The SearchContext and DocumentContext specs mention AbortController for autocomplete but don't specify cleanup on component unmount for the primary data fetches (searchDocuments, getDocument). If a user navigates away from DocumentScreen while getDocument is in-flight, the response will arrive and try to update state on an unmounted context consumer. The API client never throws (returns { ok, data, error }), but the dispatch will still fire into a potentially stale reducer.
objections.suggested_fix: Specify that every custom hook (useSearch, useDocument, useDashboardData) creates an AbortController in a useEffect and aborts on cleanup. The API client already accepts signals — the hooks must wire them. Add this as an explicit pattern in section 7 or 8.

objections.description: Request deduplication ('if an identical request is already in-flight, return the existing promise') combined with AbortController cancellation creates a race condition. If Request A is in-flight and Component B makes the same request (gets the same promise), then Component A unmounts and aborts via its signal — the shared promise is now rejected/aborted for Component B too. The dedup and abort strategies conflict.
objections.suggested_fix: Choose one: (a) Deduplicate requests but use a separate internal AbortController that only aborts when ALL consumers have aborted — reference counting. (b) Don't deduplicate; rely on the debounce in useSearch to prevent rapid duplicate requests. Option (b) is simpler and correct for this use case. Remove the deduplication claim or spec the reference-counting approach precisely.

objections.description: The spec claims '4px base, powers of 2 progression' but the scale is actually linear 4px increments (4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 32, 40, 48, 64, 80) — not powers of 2. This is fine as a spacing system but the documentation is incorrect, which will confuse developers trying to extend it.
objections.suggested_fix: Change the description to '4px base unit, incremental scale' or just remove the 'powers of 2' claim.

suggestions.description: The spec omits a critical shared component: a generic `AsyncState` or `DataRenderer` component that handles the four-state pattern (skeleton/populated/empty/error) consistently. Every screen repeats this pattern — extracting it into a shared primitive ensures consistent behavior and reduces per-screen boilerplate. Something like `<DataView status={status} skeleton={<Skeleton />} empty={<EmptyState />} error={<ErrorState onRetry={retry} />}>{(data) => <Content data={data} />}</DataView>`.

Add a `useStaggerDelay(index)` hook that returns the computed `animationDelay` for staggered list items, capped at a max total stagger (e.g., 400ms for 10+ items). Without a cap, a list of 50 items would have a 2-second stagger cascade, which feels broken.

The migration plan mentions a `/v2` prefix but doesn't specify how the frontend build handles this. Vite's `base` config would need to be `/v2/` during the transition period, which means all asset paths change. Specify that during migration, `vite.config.js` sets `base: '/v2/'` and the server mounts at `/v2/`. After cutover, revert to `base: '/'`.

No specification for touch targets. WCAG 2.5.8 (Target Size) requires 24×24px minimum with 44×44px recommended. The TabBar items, SearchResultItem tap areas, and TimelineItem tap areas should all have explicit minimum touch target sizes defined (44px recommended for mobile).

No mention of error monitoring or observability. At minimum, specify that the ErrorBoundary logs caught errors to the console with structured metadata (route, component stack) and that the API client logs failed requests with status codes. For v1 this can be console-only, but the hooks should exist for a future Sentry/equivalent integration.
